I now have all the data. Let me build the complete content abstraction into JSON files like Nexa Paraguay, starting with `es.json` as source of truth.

**Datos extraídos completamente.** Acá tenés el inventario completo de todo lo que hay actualmente en el repo de **elviajero-comercio**:

---

## 📦 Inventario Completo de Archivos de Contenido

### `/root/elviajero/content/es.json` (72KB, ~2007 líneas)
Todo el contenido en español. Keys top-level:
- `siteName`, `businessName`, `tagline`, `founded`, `navigation`
- **home** → hero, stats, features, productCatalog (32 productos, 7 categorías), gallery, testimonials (4), finalCta, newsletter, heroCarousel (6 slides), featuredProducts, bestSellers, newArrivals
- **about** → story (3 párrafos), values (4), timeline (3 items)
- **productos** → seo, hero, productCatalog (mismos 32 productos con variantes)
- **contacto** → seo, hero, info (tel, whatsapp, email, dirección, horario), form
- **faq** → seo, hero, 10 preguntas
- **tienda** → seo, hero, trustBadges
- **blog** → seo, hero, index (4 categorías, 6 posts)
- **promociones** → seo, hero, 3 promociones
- **footer** → columns (3), paymentMethods (7), trustSignals (4), social (4), contactStrip
- **whatsapp**, **cookieConsent**, **newsletter**, **paymentMethods**, **storeLocator**
- **categoryMenu** → 6 categorías con subcategorías
- **deliveryZones** → 10 zonas con tarifas
- **coupons** → 3 cupones
- **analytics**, **paymentGateway**, **supabase**, **email**, **abandonedCart**, **backInStock**

### `/root/elviajero/content/gn.json` (2.6KB)
Traducción parcial a Guaraní — navigation, home (heroCarousel minimal, stats, features, newsletter), footer, whatsapp, paymentMethods, cookieConsent

### `/root/elviajero/content/en.json` (58KB)
⚠️ **Contenido corrupto** — tiene datos de **Dayah LitWorks** (FAQ, servicios, etc.), no de El Viajero. Hay que regenerarlo.

### `/root/elviajero/content/tokens.json` (867B)
Design tokens: colores (#1B5E20 green, #E65100 orange), typography (Poppins/Inter)

### `/root/elviajero/config/site.json` (4.7KB)
Config general: dominio, locales, navegación, contacto, delivery, commerce settings, features toggle (18 features), homepage categoryIcons

### `/root/elviajero/config/promos.json` (538B)
Cupones de descuento + lifecycle config (abandoned cart timers, review request)

### `/root/elviajero/config/pages/*.json` (10 archivos)
Cada página define sus secciones con `content` keys que referencian `es.json`:
- `home.json` → 9 secciones (promo-banner, hero, stats, features, commerce-catalog, blog-index, gallery, testimonials, newsletter, cta-banner)
- `tienda.json`, `productos.json`, `contacto.json`, `faq.json`, `nosotros.json`, `blog.json`, `promociones.json`, `privacidad.json`, `terminos.json`

### Páginas con HTML hardcodeado (NO en JSON)
- **privacidad/** → 96 líneas de HTML con términos legales completos (Ley 6534/2020)
- **terminos/** → 89 líneas de HTML con términos y condiciones completos

### Blog posts (en es.json):
6 posts: guía carpa, lugares pesca, checklist camping, elegir caña, mantenimiento, destinos Paraguay

### Productos (32 en total, duplicados en 3 secciones):
Carpa, bolsa dormir, silla pescador, linterna LED, caña telescópica, chaleco, mochila 60L, cuchillo, dashcam, inflador 12V, casco moto, conservadora, parrilla, hamaca, toldo, ventilador, caja pesca, señuelos, red, boyas, botas, termo, mochila térmica, kit botiquín, eslingas, extintor, tiras remolque, guantes moto, rastreador GPS, sombrilla playa, silla playa, bebedero, comedero, kit campo

---

**Problemas detectados:**
1. `en.json` tiene datos de Dayah LitWorks (está corrupto)
2. Productos se duplican en 3 arrays (productCatalog, featuredProducts, newArrivals) — redundante
3. Privacidad y Términos están en HTML hardcodeado en las pages, no en JSON
4. Las keys de contenido se referencian con notación dot-path desde config/pages/*.json (ya compatible con Nexa pattern)

¿Arrancamos normalizando esto como en Nexa Paraguay? Mi propuesta:
1. Regenerar `es.json` eliminando duplicación de productos
2. Regenerar `en.json` (está roto)
3. Mantener `gn.json`
4. Mover privacidad y términos a JSON
5. Crear `content/pages/*.json` si querés separar page configs

Review the conversation above and update the skill library. Be ACTIVE — most sessions produce at least one skill update, even if small. A pass that does nothing is a missed learning opportunity, not a neutral outcome.

Target shape of the library: CLASS-LEVEL skills, each with a rich SKILL.md and a `references/` directory for session-specific detail. Not a long flat list of narrow one-session-one-skill entries. This shapes HOW you update, not WHETHER you update.

Signals to look for (any one of these warrants action):
  • User corrected your style, tone, format, legibility, or verbosity. Frustration signals like 'stop doing X', 'this is too verbose', 'don't format like this', 'why are you explaining', 'just give me the answer', 'you always do Y and I hate it', or an explicit 'remember this' are FIRST-CLASS skill signals, not just memory signals. Update the relevant skill(s) to embed the preference so the next session starts already knowing.
  • User corrected your workflow, approach, or sequence of steps. Encode the correction as a pitfall or explicit step in the skill that governs that class of task.
  • Non-trivial technique, fix, workaround, debugging path, or tool-usage pattern emerged that a future session would benefit from. Capture it.
  • A skill that got loaded or consulted this session turned out to be wrong, missing a step, or outdated. Patch it NOW.

Preference order — prefer the earliest action that fits, but do pick one when a signal above fired:
  1. UPDATE A CURRENTLY-LOADED SKILL. Look back through the conversation for skills the user loaded via /skill-name or you read via skill_view. If any of them covers the territory of the new learning, PATCH that one first. It is the skill that was in play, so it's the right one to extend.
  2. UPDATE AN EXISTING UMBRELLA (via skills_list + skill_view). If no loaded skill fits but an existing class-level skill does, patch it. Add a subsection, a pitfall, or broaden a trigger.
  3. ADD A SUPPORT FILE under an existing umbrella. Skills can be packaged with three kinds of support files — use the right directory per kind:
     • `references/<topic>.md` — session-specific detail (error transcripts, reproduction recipes, provider quirks) AND condensed knowledge banks: quoted research, API docs, external authoritative excerpts, or domain notes you found while working on the problem. Write it concise and for the value of the task, not as a full mirror of upstream docs.
     • `templates/<name>.<ext>` — starter files meant to be copied and modified (boilerplate configs, scaffolding, a known-good example the agent can `reproduce with modifications`).
     • `scripts/<name>.<ext>` — statically re-runnable actions the skill can invoke directly (verification scripts, fixture generators, deterministic probes, anything the agent should run rather than hand-type each time).
     Add support files via skill_manage action=write_file with file_path starting 'references/', 'templates/', or 'scripts/'. The umbrella's SKILL.md should gain a one-line pointer to any new support file so future agents know it exists.
  4. CREATE A NEW CLASS-LEVEL UMBRELLA SKILL when no existing skill covers the class. The name MUST be at the class level. The name MUST NOT be a specific PR number, error string, feature codename, library-alone name, or 'fix-X / debug-Y / audit-Z-today' session artifact. If the proposed name only makes sense for today's task, it's wrong — fall back to (1), (2), or (3).

User-preference embedding (important): when the user expressed a style/format/workflow preference, the update belongs in the SKILL.md body, not just in memory. Memory captures 'who the user is and what the current situation and state of your operations are'; skills capture 'how to do this class of task for this user'. When they complain about how you handled a task, the skill that governs that task needs to carry the lesson.

If you notice two existing skills that overlap, note it in your reply — the background curator handles consolidation at scale.

Do NOT capture (these become persistent self-imposed constraints that bite you later when the environment changes):
  • Environment-dependent failures: missing binaries, fresh-install errors, post-migration path mismatches, 'command not found', unconfigured credentials, uninstalled packages. The user can fix these — they are not durable rules.
  • Negative claims about tools or features ('browser tools do not work', 'X tool is broken', 'cannot use Y from execute_code'). These harden into refusals the agent cites against itself for months after the actual problem was fixed.
  • Session-specific transient errors that resolved before the conversation ended. If retrying worked, the lesson is the retry pattern, not the original failure.
  • One-off task narratives. A user asking 'summarize today's market' or 'analyze this PR' is not a class of work that warrants a skill.

If a tool failed because of setup state, capture the FIX (install command, config step, env var to set) under an existing setup or troubleshooting skill — never 'this tool does not work' as a standalone constraint.

'Nothing to save.' is a real option but should NOT be the default. If the session ran smoothly with no corrections and produced no new technique, just say 'Nothing to save.' and stop. Otherwise, act.
Let me review the session for skill-worthy learning.

name: nexa-paraguay-deployment
description: Build and deploy the Nexa Paraguay site to Docker Swarm on VPS 72.61.44.159
---

# Nexa Paraguay Deployment

## Current State (May 11 2026)

### Hybrid Dependencies
Sections, i18n, and client-kit stay on `.tgz` (the published `@ai-whisperers/sections@0.3.1` has a different API). All other `@ai-whisperers/*` come from GitHub Packages:
- `auth@0.2.0`, `admin@0.2.1`, `ui@0.4.0`, `seo@0.2.0`, `whatsapp@0.2.0`, `theme@0.2.0` — from npm
- `sections@0.1.0`, `i18n@0.2.0`, `client-kit@0.1.0` — from `.packages/*.tgz`

### Integrations Wired
- **HubSpot**: Contact form POSTs to `api.hsforms.com` with rate limiting + graceful fallback
- **Mailchimp**: `/api/subscribe` route with idempotent PUT + graceful degradation
- **GA4**: Measurement ID `G-XE49GLEP34` in env + DNS in CSP, gtag via Script in layout
- **Admin panel**: Dashboard at `/admin` with `AdminGuard` component (Supabase session check)
- **Content honesty**: Stats → `[Datos reales pendientes]`, testimonials → placeholders, TeamSection badges `⚠️ IA`

### Docker Build (current)
```bash
# From VPS
cd /root/nexa-paraguay
git pull origin main
docker build --no-cache -t nexa-paraguay:prod .
docker service update --force --image nexa-paraguay:prod nexa_web
```

## App Router Architecture (current)

- Framework: Next.js 16 App Router, React 19, TypeScript 5, Tailwind v4
- Routes use `[locale]/[slug]` pattern — 4 locales (es/en/nl/de), 25+ slug pages
- Content JSON-driven: `content/{locale}.json` (4 locales, `es.json` is source of truth at ~208KB), `nexa-pages/*.json` (26+ page configs), `images.json` (image registry)
- SectionsRenderer: `'use client'` component with 30+ sections loaded via `dynamic(() => import(...), { ssr: true })`
- PPR disabled (`cacheComponents` removed) — all routes are `● SSG` static HTML
- Each section component receives `pageContent` (the resolved content sub-object) and `data` (same, as fallback)
- Section content keys in page configs reference dot-paths into the locale JSON (e.g. `"home.hero"`)
- Dynamic routes using `generateStaticParams()` — all pages pre-rendered at build time
- Docker Swarm deployment with standalone output

### SectionsRenderer Content Resolution

**Critical rule for content keys in nexa-pages/*.json:**

Components in `sections.tsx` access `pageContent.KEY` (e.g., `HeroSection` does `pageContent.hero`).
Components in `FeedbackSection` use `data` directly (e.g., `data.title`).

| Component | What it reads | Correct content key |
|-----------|--------------|-------------------|
| HeroSection | `pageContent.hero` | `"home"` |
| TrustSection | `pageContent.trust` | `"home"` |
| ServicesSection | `d.services?.groups \|\| d.groups` | `"home"` |
| WhyCountrySection | `pageContent.whyCountry` | `"home"` |
| ProcessSection | `pageContent.process` | `"home"` |
| FeedbackSection | `data.title`, `data.eyebrow` | `"home.feedback"` |
| CtaBanner | `pageContent.finalCta \|\| pageContent.cta` | `"home"` |

If a section returns null, check the content key depth against what the component reads.

## Build & Deploy (current)

```bash
# Full clean rebuild (REQUIRED for SSG changes)
cd /root/nexa-paraguay
git pull origin main
docker build --no-cache -t nexa-paraguay:prod .
docker service update --force --image nexa-paraguay:prod nexa_web
sleep 5
curl -sL -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" https://nexa.paragu-ai.com
curl -sL -o /dev/null -w " admin:%{http_code}" https://nexa.paragu-ai.com/admin
echo ""
```

**CRITICAL — Always `docker build --no-cache`.** Without `--no-cache`, Docker may reuse the builder stage layer which contains the old `.next/` output even if the source files have been updated.

## Integration: HubSpot Contact Form

The contact form at `/es/contacto` POSTs to `/api/contact`, which:
1. Rate-limits by IP (10 requests/hour)
2. Sends fields (name, email, phone, message, program) to HubSpot API
3. Falls back to console.log if HubSpot is unreachable
4. Returns `{ ok: true }` regardless (never exposes errors to user)

**To verify:**
```bash
curl -s -X POST https://nexa.paragu-ai.com/api/contact \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"name":"Test","email":"[REDACTED_EMAIL]","message":"Test submission"}'
# Response: {"ok":true,"remaining":9,"hubspot":"submitted"} or "logged"
```

## Integration: Mailchimp Subscribe

The newsletter form POSTs to `/api/subscribe`, which:
1. Uses Mailchimp API v3 PUT (idempotent — safe to retry)
2. Creates subscriber with status `subscribed`
3. Gracefully handles "already subscribed" (returns ok)
4. Falls back to console.log on API failure

**Required env var:** `MAILCHIMP_AUDIENCE_ID` and `MAILCHIMP_API_KEY` in `.env.local`

## Admin Panel

Located at `/admin`. Self-contained `AdminGuard` component:
1. Creates Supabase client inline (no `@ai-whisperers/auth` dependency)
2. Checks session via `sb.auth.getSession()`
3. Redirects to `/` if no session
4. Shows stats cards for pages/content/blogs

**On first deploy**, you need a Supabase user with admin role to log in.

## SSG Cache Invalidation

Key insight from this session: **all pages produce `BAILOUT_TO_CLIENT_SIDE_RENDERING`** in their SSG HTML `<main>` tags. This is the normal behavior of a `'use client'` SectionsRenderer wrapping dynamic imports — content renders on the client via RSC hydration, not in static HTML. See the generic pitfall in `nextjs-docker-swarm` skill (pitfall #22) for the full explanation and the two fix paths (static imports vs accepting RSC serialization).

Next.js 16 SSG + Docker Swarm creates **four layers of caching** that must all be cleared:

### 1. Docker layer caching
The builder stage runs `npm run build` inside it. `--no-cache` forces Docker to re-run the builder.
```bash
docker build --no-cache -t nexa-paraguay:prod .
```

### 2. Cloudflare/edge CDN cache
```bash
curl -sL -H "Cache-Control: no-cache" https://nexa.paragu-ai.com/en/contacto
```

### 3. Browser cache
Use incognito or hard-refresh when testing.

### Verification inside container
```bash
docker exec $(docker ps -q -f name=nexa_web) cat /app/.next/server/app/en/contacto.html | grep "expected content"
```

## CRITICAL PITFALL: JSON Corruption

The `patch` tool and `sed` can corrupt large JSON files (content/es.json is 208KB, 900+ lines). A missing comma or broken key breaks the entire site (content: {} empty response — all pages render as header+footer only). **Always validate after every JSON edit:**

```bash
python3 -c "import json; json.load(open('content/es.json')); print('VALID')"
```

## .npmrc Token Management

The `.npmrc` for GitHub Packages must have a valid classic PAT (`ghp_*`). Fine-grained PATs (`github_pat_*`) do NOT work with `npm.pkg.github.com`.

**To update a stale token:**
```bash
gh auth token
# Copy the output
sed -i 's|ghp_.*|<paste token>|' .npmrc
```

**To check if the current token works:**
```bash
NPM_CONFIG_USERCONFIG=.npmrc npm view @ai-whisperers/auth version --registry=https://npm.pkg.github.com
```

## Locale Configuration Pattern

**File:** `src/lib/locales.ts` — single source of truth for locale constants.

```typescript
export const LOCALES = ['es', 'en', 'nl', 'de'] as const
export const DEFAULT_LOCALE = 'es'
export const LOCALE_COOKIE = 'NEXT_LOCALE'
export type Locale = (typeof LOCALES)[number]
export const LOCALE_FLAGS: Record<string, string> = {
  es: '/images/flags/es.svg',
  en: '/images/flags/en.svg',
  nl: '/images/flags/nl.svg',
  de: '/images/flags/de.svg',
}
```

**tsconfig.json must use `./src/*` path:**
```json
"paths": { "@/*": ["./src/*"] }
```

## Content Architecture: Homepage vs Dedicated Pages

**Critical rule: NO duplicated content between homepage and dedicated pages.**

Each piece of content has ONE owner:

| Content | Owner Page | Homepage Shows |
|---|---|---|
| Programs tiers | /programas | Summary headline + CTA link |
| Services groups | /servicios | Summary headline + CTA link |
| Why Paraguay pillars | /por-que-paraguay | Summary headline + CTA link |
| Process steps | /proceso | Summary headline + CTA link |
| Testimonials | /casos-de-exito | CTA link only |
| Hero, Stats, Trust, Tax Calc, Benelux | / (homepage) | Full content (unique) |

## Living Upgrade Plan

The full 75-idea upgrade plan for Nexa Paraguay lives at:
**https://github.com/Ai-Whisperers/infrastructure/blob/main/nexa-paraguay-upgrade-plan.md**
", "path": "nexa-paraguay-deployment/SKILL.md", "skill_dir": "/root/.hermes/skills/nexa-paraguay-deployment", "linked_files": {"references": ["references/monorepo-architecture.md", "references/docker-build-monorepo.md", "references/adding-new-section-component.md", "references/section-registration-pitfall.md", "references/content-data-pollution-fix.md"], "scripts": ["scripts/clean-json-pollution.py"]}, "usage_hint": "To view linked files, call skill_view(name, file_path) where file_path is e.g. 'references/api.md' or 'assets/config.yaml'", "required_environment_variables": [], "required_commands": [], "missing_required_environment_variables": [], "missing_credential_files": [], "missing_required_commands": [], "setup_needed": false, "setup_skipped": false, "readiness_status": "available"}

Assumes a SectionsRenderer-style architecture with data-agnostic component rendering.", "tags": [], "related_skills": [], "content": "---
name: content-to-database-migration
description: >-
  Migrate JSON-driven Next.js content sites (App or Pages Router) from
  filesystem-based JSON storage to Supabase/Postgres. Covers data model
  design (single-table vs normalized), storage migration (images, manifests),

  and phased rollout. Assumes a SectionsRenderer-style architecture with

  version: "1.0"
  tags: [database, supabase, migration, content, architecture, postgres, nextjs]
triggers:

# Content-to-Database Migration

Migrate a JSON-driven Next.js site from filesystem storage to Supabase/Postgres. This is a data-layer-only change — the SectionsRenderer, section components, and page config system remain untouched.

## Architecture Overview

### Pre-migration (file-based)

```
JSON files on disk (content/, nexa-pages/, images.json)
  → loadPageData() reads via readFileSync
    → SectionsRenderer receives {content, pageConfig, images}
      → Section component renders HTML
```

### Post-migration (Supabase)

```
Supabase tenant_config table (single row per tenant)
  → Supabase Storage CDN (images)
    → loadPageData() queries via @supabase/supabase-js
      → SectionsRenderer receives {content, pageConfig, images}
        → Section component renders HTML
```

**Key insight:** The renderer is data-agnostic. Only the loader changes. Zero section components need modification.

## Data Model Design

### Option A: Single-Table (Recommended for <20 tenants, SSG builds)

One row per tenant. All content in JSONB columns.

```sql
create table tenant_config (
  id uuid primary key default gen_random_uuid(),
  slug text unique not null,
  site jsonb not null default '{}',               -- site.json
  content jsonb not null default '{}',             -- all locales, nested: { es: {...}, en: {...} }
  page_configs jsonb not null default '{}',        -- all pages, keyed by slug: { home: {...}, faq: {...} }
  images_manifest jsonb not null default '{}',     -- images.json structure
  testimonials jsonb default '{}',
  created_at timestamptz default now(),
  updated_at timestamptz default now()
);
```

`content` shape:
```json
{
  "es": { "siteName": "...", "navigation": {...}, "home": {...}, "faqPage": {...}, ... },
  "en": { ... },
  "nl": { ... },
  "de": { ... }
}
```

`page_configs` shape:
```json
{
  "home": { "id": "home", "sections": [...] },
  "servicios": { "id": "servicios", "sections": [...] },
  "faq": { ... },
  "sobre": { ... }
}
```

**Pros:** One query, zero joins. ~500KB per tenant (Postgres jsonb limit is ~1GB). GIN index on slug for instant lookup.

**Cons:** Whole column must be read/written. Not suitable for partial content updates via admin panel without merging.

### Option B: Normalized (Recommended for runtime CMS + admin panel)

Separate tables for content types.

```sql
create table tenants (
  id uuid primary key default gen_random_uuid(),
  slug text unique not null,
  name text,
  default_locale text not null,
  locales text[] not null,
  site jsonb not null default '{}',
  images_manifest jsonb not null default '{}',
  created_at timestamptz default now()
);

create table tenant_content (
  id uuid primary key default gen_random_uuid(),
  tenant_id uuid references tenants(id) on delete cascade,
  locale text not null,
  page_slug text not null,           -- 'home', 'servicios', null = global
  data jsonb not null,
  updated_at timestamptz default now(),
  unique(tenant_id, locale, page_slug)
);

create table tenant_images (
  id uuid primary key default gen_random_uuid(),
  tenant_id uuid references tenants(id) on delete cascade,
  category text not null,            -- 'hero', 'brand', 'trust'
  key text not null,                 -- 'localizedEs', 'certificate'
  src text not null,
  alt text,
  alt_by_locale jsonb,
  mobile_src text,
  unique(tenant_id, category, key)
);
```

**Pros:** Partial updates possible. Supabase Realtime-friendly. Easy admin editing (single page at a time).

**Cons:** Multiple queries per request. More complex loader. Joins or N+1 risk.

### Option C: Hybrid (What most projects actually need)

Single `tenant_config` row for SSG builds (Option A), but also store blog posts in a separate `blog_posts` table for the admin panel:

```sql
create table blog_posts (
  id uuid primary key default gen_random_uuid(),
  tenant_id uuid references tenants(id) on delete cascade,
  locale text not null,
  slug text not null,
  title text not null,
  excerpt text,
  body text,                         -- markdown body
  image text,
  tags text[],
  published boolean default false,
  created_at timestamptz default now(),
  updated_at timestamptz default now(),
  unique(tenant_id, locale, slug)
);
```

The main content JSON stays in `tenant_config` for fast SSG. Blog posts are queryable individually.

## Image Migration (Supabase Storage)

### Bucket Structure

Single bucket, tenant-prefixed paths:
```
nexa-images/
  nexa-paraguay/
    brand/logo.webp
    hero/hero-bg.webp
    trust/certificate.webp
  elviajero/
    ...
```

Or bucket per tenant (cleaner RBAC with RLS):
```
nexa-paraguay-images/
elviajero-images/
```

### Script: Migrate 290 images to Supabase Storage

```bash
#!/bin/bash
SUPABASE_URL="https://qyvokpribmbrosafntqa.supabase.co"
SERVICE_KEY="sb_secret_..."

MIGRATE_DIR="/root/nexa-paraguay/public/images"
BUCKET="nexa-images"
PREFIX="nexa-paraguay"

for dir in brand hero trust process programs team testimonials blog press; do
  for src in "$MIGRATE_DIR/$dir"/*; do
    [ -f "$src" ] || continue
    fname=$(basename "$src")
    dest="$PREFIX/$dir/$fname"
    
    curl -s -X POST "$SUPABASE_URL/storage/v1/object/$BUCKET/$dest" \
      -H "Authorization: Bearer $SERVICE_KEY" \
      -H "Content-Type: $(file --mime-type -b "$src")" \
      --data-binary @"$src"
    
    echo "$src -> $dest"
  done
done
```

### Update images manifest

After migration, rebuild `images.json` with Supabase Storage URLs:

```python
import json

SUPABASE_URL = "https://qyvokpribmbrosafntqa.supabase.co"
BUCKET = "nexa-images"
PREFIX = "nexa-paraguay"
BASE_URL = f"{SUPABASE_URL}/storage/v1/object/public/{BUCKET}/{PREFIX}"

with open("images.json") as f:
    manifest = json.load(f)

def transform_path(path):
    """Convert /images/hero/bg.webp -> full Supabase URL"""
    relative = path.lstrip("/images/")
    return f"{BASE_URL}/{relative}"

for category, entries in manifest.get("images", {}).items():
    for key, entry in entries.items():
        if isinstance(entry, dict) and "src" in entry:
            entry["src"] = transform_path(entry["src"])
        if isinstance(entry, dict) and "mobileSrc" in entry:
            entry["mobileSrc"] = transform_path(entry["mobileSrc"])
        if isinstance(entry, str):
            manifest["images"][category][key] = transform_path(entry)

with open("images-supabase.json", "w") as f:
    json.dump(manifest, f, indent=2)
```

## Generated-Data-Module Architecture (Build-Time Code Generation)

Some sites (notably the Paragu-AI Builder at `/tmp/paragu-ai-builder/web/`) use a **pre-build code generation step** instead of runtime `readFileSync`:

```
sites/<slug>/*.json  →  scripts/generate-tenant-data.ts  →  lib/engine/generated/tenant-data.ts (51K lines, 48 tenants)
                                                              →  lib/engine/generated/tenants/<slug>.<locale>.ts
```

The loaders (`site-loader.ts`, `images-loader.ts`) perform **synchronous object lookups** against the generated module — no `fs` calls. This makes them portable between Node and Edge Runtime.

```typescript
// lib/engine/site-loader.ts
import { CONTENT, IMAGES_MANIFESTS, PAGES, SITES } from './generated/tenant-data'

export function loadSite(slug: string): SiteDefinition {
  const raw = SITES[slug]  // synchronous object lookup, no fs
  if (!raw) throw new Error(`Site not found: ${slug}`)
  return { ...raw, slug }
}
```

### Migration strategy for generated-data-module architecture

When migrating from generated modules to Supabase, the approach differs from file-reader migration:

1. **Keep the `generate-tenant-data.ts` generator as a fallback** — it still generates the module during build
2. **Add a Supabase-backed loader with the same interface** so the composed-site pipeline doesn't change
3. **Phase in with a feature flag**: the loader checks a boolean or env var, then reads from Supabase OR the generated module
4. **Remove the generator only after Phase 3** (multi-tenant admin) is stable

The loader interface to preserve (already async-compatible since `generateStaticParams` supports async):

```typescript
// Keep this interface identical, swap implementation underneath
export function loadSite(slug: string): SiteDefinition  // stays sync
export async function loadSiteFromDb(slug: string): Promise<SiteDefinition> // new
```

### SSG vs dynamic render mode for tenant pages

The Paragu-AI Builder already uses `export const dynamic = 'force-dynamic'` on the tenant catch-all page because commerce sections (`commerce-catalog`, `featured-products`) use Supabase admin client internally which calls `cookies()` — forbidden during SSG. This means:

- Tenant pages already render at request time (SSR), not at build time (SSG)
- A Supabase-backed loader adds zero architectural friction — the DB query runs every request anyway
- The `force-dynamic` export was already necessary before the data migration (commerce sections were the trigger)
- If migrating a site WITHOUT commerce, SSG can be preserved: Supabase queries only run during `next build`, static files serve from CDN

Check `page.tsx` for `export const dynamic` before deciding the render mode. If already `force-dynamic`, the DB query cost is already paid.

## Loader Refactoring

```typescript
// src/lib/supabase-loader.ts
import { createClient } from '@supabase/supabase-js'

const supabase = createClient(
  process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL!,
  process.env.SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY!
)

// LRU cache for server-side dedup (React.cache not available in Pages Router)
const dataCache = new Map<string, { data: any; expires: number }>()

export async function loadTenantData(slug: string) {
  const cacheKey = `tenant:${slug}`
  const cached = dataCache.get(cacheKey)
  if (cached && Date.now() < cached.expires) return cached.data

  const { data, error } = await supabase
    .from('tenant_config')
    .select('site, content, page_configs, images_manifest, testimonials')
    .eq('slug', slug)
    .single()

  if (error) {
    console.error('[Supabase] loadTenantData error:', error)
    return null
  }

  dataCache.set(cacheKey, { data, expires: Date.now() + 60_000 })
  return data
}

export async function loadPageData(locale: string, slug: string) {
  const tenantSlug = process.env.TENANT_SLUG || 'nexa-paraguay'
  const tenantData = await loadTenantData(tenantSlug)
  if (!tenantData) return null

  const localeContent = tenantData.content[locale] || tenantData.content['es']
  const pageConfig = tenantData.page_configs?.[slug] || null
  const images = tenantData.images_manifest?.images || null

  return { content: localeContent, pageConfig, images, pageId: slug, locale }
}
```

### For `generateStaticParams` (SSG builds)

The SSG build needs to read the tenant slug and available pages. Since `generateStaticParams` runs at build time, the Supabase query happens once:

```typescript
// src/app/[locale]/[slug]/page.tsx
export async function generateStaticParams() {
  const tenantData = await loadTenantData(process.env.TENANT_SLUG || 'nexa-paraguay')
  const pageSlugs = Object.keys(tenantData?.page_configs || {})
  const locales = Object.keys(tenantData?.content || {})

  const params: { locale: string; slug: string }[] = []
  for (const locale of locales) {
    for (const slug of pageSlugs) {
      params.push({ locale, slug })
    }
  }
  return params
}
```

## Caching Strategy

### Layer 1: In-memory LRU (server-side, 60s TTL)

```typescript
// src/lib/lru-cache.ts
const MAX_ENTRIES = 20
const TTL = 60_000

const cache = new Map<string, { data: any; expires: number }>()

export function cachedQuery<T>(key: string, fetchFn: () => Promise<T>): Promise<T> {
  const existing = cache.get(key)
  if (existing && Date.now() < existing.expires) {
    return Promise.resolve(existing.data)
  }
  return fetchFn().then(data => {
    if (cache.size >= MAX_ENTRIES) {
      const firstKey = cache.keys().next().value
      if (firstKey) cache.delete(firstKey)
    }
    cache.set(key, { data, expires: Date.now() + TTL })
    return data
  })
}
```

### Layer 2: React.cache() (Server Components only)

```typescript
// In app router, use React.cache to deduplicate within the same render pass
import { cache } from 'react'

export const getTenantData = cache(async (slug: string) => {
  return loadTenantData(slug)
})
```

### Layer 3: SSG (build-time only, fastest)

For fully static sites, all Supabase queries happen during `next build`. The generated HTML/JSON files are static assets served from CDN. Zero DB queries at runtime.

### Layer 4: ISR (for runtime content updates)

```typescript
export const revalidate = 3600  // rebuild every hour

// Or webhook-triggered revalidation:
// POST /api/revalidate?secret=TOKEN&path=/es
```

## Migration Strategy (3 Phases)

### Phase 1: Files → Database (1 day)

1. Create the SQL migration for `tenant_config`
2. Run a script that reads all JSON files and inserts as one row
3. Test locally: `loadPageData()` from Supabase returns same data as files
4. Create a feature flag: `if (USE_SUPABASE) { ... } else { ... }`
5. Deploy with Supabase loader. Verify all pages render identically.

### Phase 2: Images → Storage (1 day)

1. Create Supabase Storage bucket (public or authenticated)
2. Run 290-image upload script
3. Generate updated images manifest with Storage URLs
4. Update the `images_manifest` column in `tenant_config`
5. Verify all images render in production

### Phase 3: Admin Dashboard + Multi-Tenant (1-2 weeks)

1. Create `/admin/content` page with form-based editor
2. Add `PUT /api/admin/content` API route
3. Add `POST /api/revalidate` for ISR
4. Edge function for content updates → revalidation
5. Resolve tenant slug from domain/Host header
6. Single deploy serves all clients

## Admin Panel API Route (Phase 3)

```typescript
// src/app/api/admin/content/route.ts
import { createClient } from '@supabase/supabase-js'
import { NextResponse } from 'next/server'
import { revalidatePath } from 'next/cache'

const supabase = createClient(
  process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL!,
  process.env.SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY!
)

export async function GET() {
  const slug = process.env.TENANT_SLUG || 'nexa-paraguay'
  const { data } = await supabase
    .from('tenant_config')
    .select('content')
    .eq('slug', slug)
    .single()
  return NextResponse.json(data?.content || {})
}

export async function PUT(request: Request) {
  const slug = process.env.TENANT_SLUG || 'nexa-paraguay'
  const body = await request.json()

  const { locale, page_slug, data } = body
  // For single-table: read current, merge, write
  const { data: current } = await supabase
    .from('tenant_config')
    .select('content')
    .eq('slug', slug)
    .single()

  current.content[locale] = {
    ...current.content[locale],
    [page_slug]: { ...current.content[locale]?.[page_slug], ...data }
  }

  await supabase
    .from('tenant_config')
    .update({ content: current.content, updated_at: new Date() })
    .eq('slug', slug)

  // Trigger ISR revalidation
  const paths = [`/${locale}/${page_slug}`, `/${locale}`]
  for (const path of paths) {
    revalidatePath(path, 'page')
  }

  return NextResponse.json({ ok: true })
}
```

### Payload Too Large for CLI (E2BIG)

When the SQL migration payload exceeds ~100KB (common with full content JSONB), curl's CLI argument fails with `Argument list too long`.

**Fix:** Write the payload JSON to a temp file, pass via `-d @/tmp/payload.json`. The SQL query is embedded inside the JSON payload's `query` field.

```python
# Write payload to file to avoid E2BIG
payload = {"query": f"INSERT INTO tenant_config ... VALUES ('slug', $json${json.dumps(content)}$json$::jsonb, ...)"}
with open('/tmp/payload.json', 'w') as f:
    json.dump(payload, f)

# Execute via curl with @file syntax
subprocess.run(["curl", "-s", "-X", "POST", url, "-H", "Authorization: Bearer {PAT}", "-H", "Content-Type: application/json", "-d", "@/tmp/payload.json"])
```

### Supabase Storage Upload — Skip HEAD Check

Batch HEAD checks against Supabase Storage time out at scale (289 files). Upload directly with `x-upsert: true` header and a `-m 120` timeout for files >2MB.

### Image Upload Timeout for Large Files

Files >2MB (WebP hero images, blog photos) need `curl -m 120` (120s timeout). Default curl timeout (30s) can fail on Supabase Storage for large uploads during batch uploads.

### CSP Must Allow Supabase Domain

If images or API calls use Supabase URLs, add the project URL to both `img-src` and `connect-src` in CSP headers. The URL format is `https://{project-ref}.supabase.co`.

### Supabase Key Format: sb_* vs JWT

Supabase now issues keys in `sb_secret_*` and `sb_publishable_*` format. These work directly with `createClient(url, key)`. The old JWT format still works for the REST `apikey` header. When building Docker images, hardcode the anon key (safe, it's public) and pass the service role key via runtime env.

## Pitfalls

- **SSG + Supabase queries at build time**: The `generateStaticParams` function runs `next build`, which has read access to Supabase. But the build server must have network access to Supabase (it does, since we already use Supabase from the VPS). Test with `node -e "require('@supabase/supabase-js')"` in the Docker build context.

- **Cache invalidation is four-layer**: (1) Local `.next/` build cache — `rm -rf .next` required. (2) Docker layer caching — `docker build --no-cache` required. (3) Cloudflare/edge CDN — purge or reduce TTL. (4) Browser cache — hard refresh.

- **`readFileSync` is sync, Supabase queries are async**: The current `loadPageData()` is synchronous. Switching to `async` means:
  - Pages Router: `getServerSideProps` supports async — fine.
  - App Router: Server components support async — fine.
  - `generateStaticParams`: supports async — fine.
  - But any synchronous code that calls `loadPageData` will need to be wrapped.

- **Supabase anon key vs service role key in Docker**: If the Docker image is shared (you push to a registry), the anon key is safe in build args (it's public). The service role key must NOT be in build args — pass it at runtime via `docker service update --env-add` or docker-compose environment.

- **`write_file` truncates long JWT keys**: When writing Supabase keys to `.env` or code files, the tool silently truncates strings >100 chars. Use the terminal tool: `echo "KEY=sb_secret_..." >> .env` or set via `docker service update --env-add`.

- **Image CDN URL format**: Supabase Storage URLs follow `{SUPABASE_URL}/storage/v1/object/public/{BUCKET}/{PATH}`. The CDN may add `?download=` parameter — normalize in the loader to strip it.

- **Storage bucket must be public** for images referenced in content (or add proper RLS). `curl -I $URL` returns `200` with public bucket, `401` without.

- **Content merging on update**: When updating a single page via the admin panel, you must read the existing `content` JSONB, merge the specific page's data, and write the whole column back. Two concurrent edits can race. Use `SELECT ... FOR UPDATE` or Supabase's `rpc` with row-level locking for production admin usage.

- **GenerateStaticParams with dynamic blog posts**: If blog posts are stored in `content[locale].blog.posts`, the `generateStaticParams` must read this array and generate `{locale, slug}` pairs for every post. This adds build time linearly with post count.

- **File size before migration**: Total active JSON content is ~964KB across 4 locales + 26 page configs + images.json + testimonials.json. The `tenant_config` row fits well within jsonb limits.

- **Image total is 154MB across 290 files**: Supabase Storage free tier has 1GB storage + 5GB bandwidth. This fits. Pro tier ($25/mo) has 100GB bandwidth. For 22+ client sites, Pro is necessary.

- **Backward compat during Phase 1**: Keep the JSON files on disk as a fallback. The feature flag `USE_SUPABASE` lets you toggle between loaders. Only remove files after Phase 3 is stable.

- **Migration script must handle image format changes**: The current `images.json` has an `images` wrapper (`{ tenant, basePath, images: { ... } }`). When migrating, the `images` key maps to `images_manifest.images` in the `tenant_config`. Pass `data.images_manifest.images` (not `data.images_manifest`) to `SectionsRenderer` — or flatten in the loader.

- **`generateStaticParams` returns empty for blog post slugs**: If blog posts are inside the content JSON (`content.es.blog.posts[]`), the loader must extract all post slugs during SSG. Cache this query result so it doesn't re-fetch for every page.

# Next.js Content Audit & Standardization

## When to Use

A content-heavy Next.js site where:
- JSON content files exist (content/es.json, images.json) but pages show empty shells
- Generic fallback renderer shows only headings, ignores items/paragraphs/members/pillars
- 25+ page configs reference sections with no matching component
- Data key names vary across pages (q/a vs pregunta/respuesta vs term/definition)
- Theming uses inline hex values repeated 200+ times
- No caching, no error boundaries, 50+ `: any` types

## Detection

### 1. Content-Exists-but-Not-Rendering Audit

```bash
# For each page config, check if the content key actually resolves
cd /root/project
for f in nexa-pages/*.json; do
  slug=$(python3 -c "import json; d=json.load(open('$f')); print(d.get('slug','unknown'))")
  python3 -c "
import json
d_content = json.load(open('content/es.json'))
d_config = json.load(open('$f'))
for sec in d_config.get('sections',[]):
    content_key = sec.get('content','')
    if content_key:
        parts = content_key.split('.')
        obj = d_content
        for p in parts:
            obj = obj.get(p, {}) if isinstance(obj, dict) else None
        status = 'OK' if obj else 'MISSING'
        print(f'{sec[\"id\"]:20s} → {content_key:30s} [{status}]')
  " 2>/dev/null
done
```

### 2. Component-to-Section Mapping Gap Analysis

```bash
# Extract all section IDs from page configs
grep -roh '"id": "[^"]*"' nexa-pages/*.json | sort -u | while read line; do
  id=$(echo "$line" | cut -d'"' -f4)
  if ! grep -q "'$id'" src/pages/\\[slug\\].tsx src/pages/index.tsx 2>/dev/null; then
    echo "MISSING COMPONENT: $id"
  fi
done
```

## Core Architecture Pattern

### Three-tier rendering pipeline:

```
Page Config (nexa-pages/*.json)
  → SECTION_MAP (src/pages/[slug].tsx)
    → Section Component (src/components/sections*.tsx)
      → Content JSON (content/*.json, images.json)
```

### Section Component Contract

Every section component should accept these props:
```tsx
interface SectionComponentProps {
  variant?: string
  pageContent: Record<string, any>  // reconstructed as {keyName: data}
  data?: any        // the resolved section data (preferred)
  images?: any      // images.json manifest
  [key: string]: any
}
```

Component pattern:
```tsx
export function MySection({ pageContent, data, images }: SectionComponentProps) {
  const d = data || pageContent || {}  // data is preferred
  const items = d.items || []
  if (!items.length) return null       // early return if empty
  return (<section>...</section>)
}
```

### Section Dispatch (in page file) — CRITICAL PAGE CONTEXT FIX

The dispatch must reconstruct `pageContent` so that components looking up by content key name get the correct data. **Do NOT pass `sectionData || content` as pageContent directly** — section components look up `pageContent.hero`, not `pageContent` as the hero data itself.

**The one-line bug that silences all content:**
```tsx
// BROKEN — pageContent = the resolved sub-object (e.g. content.home.hero)
// But HeroSection does pageContent.hero which is content.home.hero.hero = null
<Comp pageContent={sectionData || content} />
```

**Two valid fixes:**

*Fix A — Use `pageContent` as the parent object:*
If the component expects `pageContent.hero` (most do), set `content: "home"` in the page config section, then `pageContent = content.home`. The component's `pageContent.hero` resolves to `content.home.hero` ✅.

*Fix B — Reconstruct pageContent:*
```tsx
const keyName = section.content?.split('.').pop() || section.id
const pc: any = {}
pc[keyName] = sectionData || content
<Comp pageContent={pc} data={sectionData} />
```
This wraps the section data in an object with the key name the component expects. Component does `pageContent.hero` → `pc.hero` → `sectionData` ✅.

**Key rule:** Components that accept both `pageContent` and `data` props:
- `pageContent` → the parent object that the component scans with `pageContent.hero`, `pageContent.trust`, etc.
- `data` → the specifically resolved sub-section data for components that use it directly (FeedbackSection, GenericSection)

```tsx
const Component = SECTION_MAP[section.id]
if (Component) {
  const sectionData = resolveContent(content, section.content || section.id)
  const pc: any = {}
  const keyName = section.content?.split('.').pop() || section.id
  pc[keyName] = sectionData || content
  return <ErrorBoundary key={key} name={section.id}>
    <Component pageContent={pc} data={sectionData} images={images} />
  </ErrorBoundary>
}
```

The `keyName` extracts the last segment of the content key (e.g. `home.hero` → `hero`, `faqPage.full` → `full`) which matches what the component looks up.

This pattern must be applied in BOTH `[slug].tsx` AND `index.tsx` — they share the same dispatch logic but are separate page files.

### Generic Fallback Renderer

For sections without dedicated components, render content generically:
- `data.items || data.full?.items || data.groups || data.pillars || data.members || data.paragraphs || data.trust?.items`
- `data.body || data.content`
- Headline, subheadline, eyebrow, CTA button
- Image resolution for memberImage and imageUrl

### Content Duplication Detection

When a dedicated page (e.g. `/servicios`) renders the same service/program content as the homepage, the issue is usually **content data duplication** in the JSON files, not in the rendering logic:

1. `home.services` and `servicesPage.services` both define the same 3 groups and 9 services
2. The page configs reference different content paths (`home.services` on homepage, `servicesPage.services` on servicios page)
3. Each resolves correctly, but the content under those paths is structurally identical

**Detection:**

```bash
# Compare two content paths from the JSON
python3 -c "
import json
d = json.load(open('content/en.json'))
h = d.get('home',{}).get('services',{})
s = d.get('servicesPage',{}).get('services',{})
h_groups = [g['title'] for g in h.get('groups',[])]
s_groups = [g['title'] for g in s.get('groups',[])]
print('home.services groups:', h_groups)
print('servicesPage.services groups:', s_groups)
print('SAME:', h_groups == s_groups)
"
```

**Full cross-page scan for all section types:**

```python
def content_signature(obj):
    \"\"\"Create a compact structural signature for comparison.\"\"\"
    if isinstance(obj, dict):
        keys = sorted(k for k in obj.keys() if k not in ('seo', 'ctaHref', 'image', 'imageUrl', 'icon'))
        return tuple((k, content_signature(obj[k])) for k in keys)
    elif isinstance(obj, list):
        return tuple(content_signature(o) for o in obj[:3])
    return str(obj)[:60]

# Check two content paths for identical structure
h_sig = content_signature(d['home']['services'])
s_sig = content_signature(d['servicesPage']['services'])
print('IDENTICAL:', h_sig == s_sig)  # True means the data is the same under different paths
```

Also scan for **same-component intersections**: which section IDs appear across multiple pages, and which of those have identical content signatures.

```python
section_usage = {}
for page_id, config in all_pages.items():
    for sec in config.get('sections', []):
        sid = sec.get('id')
        if sid not in section_usage: section_usage[sid] = []
        section_usage[sid].append(page_id)
# Then filter to len >= 2
```

**Fix options:**
- **Option A: Remove from one page** — Remove the section from the homepage; the dedicated page covers it with more detail
- **Option B: Differentiate content** — Make the homepage version show fundamentally different content (icons/benefits summary vs detailed ✓ lists), using the same keys but shorter descriptions
- **Option C: Component-level dedup** — If the same component renders from different content paths but the data is identical, have the dedicated page config reference the homepage's content path directly

### Dedicated section types to always check for duplication

| Section ID | Typical duplicate pattern | Fix preference |
|---|---|---|
| `services` / `services-detail` | Homepage 9-card grid vs /servicios detailed page | Remove from homepage |
| `process-timeline` | Homepage 5-step timeline vs /proceso | Remove from homepage or replace with teaser |
| `programs-comparison` | Homepage 3-4 tier cards vs /programas | Remove from homepage |
| `cta-banner` | Same "Take the first step" CTA across 6+ pages | Make each page's CTA text unique |
| `trust-signals` | Same "One program/One trip" across 3+ pages | Differentiate content per page |
| `story` | Same "Our Story" on /sobre and /fundador | Give founder a personal bio instead |
| `newsletter-signup` | Same form on /blog and /recursos | Acceptable — intentional redundancy |
| `why-destination` | Same 3 pillars on home and dedicated | Minor — usually different enough |

## Data Key Standardization

### Common content structures in Spanish JSON files

| Content Type | Keys (preferred) | Keys (fallback) |
|-------------|-----------------|-----------------|
| FAQ items | `q`, `a` | `pregunta`, `respuesta`, `question`, `answer` |
| Glossary | `term`, `definition` | `q`, `a`, `title`, `description` |
| Team members | `name`, `role`, `description`, `memberImage` | `image`, `imageUrl` |
| Blog posts | `slug`, `title`, `excerpt`, `date`, `image`, `body` | `coverImage`, `author` |
| Pillars | `title`, `description`, `imageUrl`, `bullets` | `icon` |
| Services | `title`, `description` (grouped in `groups[]`) | `icon`, `price` |
| Process steps | `number`, `title`, `description`, `duration`, `image` | `time` |
| Stats | `value`, `label` | `number`, `description` |
| Privacy items | `q`, `a` | `title`, `body` |
| Testimonials | `name`, `quote`, `rating`, `image` | `author`, `text` |
| Gallery | `images[{src, alt, caption}]` | `items` |

### Standardize to prefer `q`/`a` for FAQ-like content

Spanish content files consistently use `q` and `a` as keys (shorter JSON = smaller bundle). Component fallback should check these first:

```tsx
const question = item.q || item.pregunta || item.question || item.title
const answer = item.a || item.respuesta || item.answer || item.description || item.body
```

grep -o '#[0-9A-Fa-f]\\{6\\}' src/components/*.tsx | sort -u
